subject: [PATCH v3] memcg: cache obj_stock by memcg, not by objcg pointer

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary: Author suggests dropping the patch from mm-tree, citing that the multi-objcg series will address the issue in a future release and can be backported to non-LTS versions.
reviews.raw_body:
Here is my suggestion on how to proceed.

1. Let's drop this patch (memcg: cache obj_stock by memcg, not by objcg pointer)
   from mm-tree.

2. Let's not add anything to 7.1.

3. 7.2+ will have the multi-objcg series [1] and the patches will have fixes
   tag. If someone uses non-LTS 7.1, they can easily find those patches and can
   backport them.

Please let me know if there are any concerns.


[1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260522011908.1669332-1-shakeel.butt@linux.dev/

2026-05-22.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory control group (memcg) implementation, specifically when dealing with cross-NUMA workloads. The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.

The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.
